title: Borrowed Time
dek: Russian novelist. Survivor of a staged execution.
mortality_event: 1849 staged execution / mock firing squad at Semyonovsky Square
citable_summary: Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoyevsky (1821–1881) was a Russian novelist whose 1849 mock execution and Siberian imprisonment became decisive facts in his later fiction.
Dostoyevsky was arrested over the Petrashevsky Circle, sentenced to death, taken to a firing-squad ritual, and reprieved only at the last moment before being sent to Siberia.
This obituary comic treats the reprieve as borrowed time: the later novels return again and again to freedom, guilt, punishment, faith, and life under extreme moral pressure.
story_notes: Dostoyevsky's near-execution was staged as punishment before exile, but he experienced it as an immediate confrontation with death. That moment became a recurring imaginative source in his mature work.
The comic follows the path from early literary promise to arrest, the square, the lowered guns, prison at Omsk, and the later novels that turned punishment into artistic material.

The source trail combines a reference account of the arrest, an essay on Dostoyevsky's letters and post-prison work, a public-domain text from his prison literature, and a Literary Hub essay on the mock execution's afterlife in the novels.
page_summaries: Opening page introducing Dostoyevsky and the idea of borrowed time.
Early life near suffering and institutions of poverty.
The young writer resists the uniform path expected of him.
Poor Folk opens the possibility of literary life.
Rooms, circles, and forbidden conversations draw state attention.
The arrest closes around the Petrashevsky Circle.
Semyonovsky Square becomes the stage for a firing-squad ritual.
The guns lower after a last-minute reprieve.
Life inside punishment replaces the expected death.
Omsk prison becomes the brutal school behind later fiction.
Books and memory survive the furnace of exile.
The soul goes on trial in the later novels.
St. Petersburg and 1881 frame the end of the life.
Closing page on the work that remains after borrowed time.
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.
